<iframe src="https://widgets.example.com/widget-a"></iframe>

Pros:Complete isolation
Cons: Performance overhead, difficult communication, styling inconsistency, accessibility issues

 

Option B: Native Web Components

customElements.define('analytics-widget', class extends HTMLElement {});

Pros: Browser-native, framework-agnostic
Cons: No SAPUI5 features, reinvent data binding/routing/i18n, steep learning curve

 

Option C: UI5 Components

import Component from "sap/ui/core/Component";

const oComponent = await Component.create({ name: "com.example.widget" });

Pros: Full SAPUI5 capabilities
Cons: Complex lifecycle management, no standard metadata format

 

Option D : UI5 Integration Cards - Selected

Manifest-based configuration:

{
    "sap.app": {
        "id": "com.example.ordervalue",
        "type": "card"
    },
    "sap.card": {
        "type": "Component",
        "header": {
            "title": "Order Value at Risk"
        },
        "content": {
            "componentName": "com.example.ordervalue"
        }
    }
}

Dashboard loads dynamically:

import Card from "sap/ui/integration/widgets/Card";

const oCard = new Card({
    manifest: "https://widgets.example.com/kpi-widget1/manifest.json",
    baseUrl: "https://widgets.example.com/kpi-widget1"
});
oCard.placeAt("container");

 

Why Integration Cards: 

Standardized format - Official SAPUI5 specification

Support Two card types:

  • Component cards: Full custom code for complex widgets
  • Declarative cards: Manifest-only (List, Table, Analytical, ...)

Built-in features: Headers, data binding, actions, error handling

Parameter passing: card.setParameters() with auto-refresh

Framework alignment: Native SAPUI5, team expertise, Fiori guidelines

 

Architecture Decision 3: Communication Patterns

The Challenge: Bidirectional Communication

Two-way communication required:

  1. Dashboard → Widgets: Apply filters to all widgets
  2. Widgets → Dashboard: Handle user clicks (cross-filtering, navigation)

 

Evaluated Patterns

Pattern 1: Pure EventBus

import EventBus from "sap/ui/core/EventBus";

// SAP UI5 EventBus (pub/sub communication)
const eventBus = EventBus.getInstance();

eventBus.publish("filters", "changed", {
  timeRange: "last7days" // For example
});

Pros: Loose coupling
Cons: State scattered in events

 

Pattern 2: State-Driven Reactive 

import JSONModel from "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel";

// SAP UI5 JSON Model (client-side state)
const filterModel = new JSONModel({
  timeRange: "last7days"
});

Pros: Single source of truth
Cons: Integration Cards don't support reactive binding

 

Pattern 3: Host-Controlled

import Card from "sap/ui/integration/widgets/Card";

// SAP UI5 Integration Cards - trigger data refresh
cards.forEach((card) => card.refresh());

Pros: Centralized control
Cons: Tight coupling

 

Our Solution: Hybrid EventBus + State-Driven

Best of both worlds:

import JSONModel from "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel";
import EventBus from "sap/ui/core/EventBus";

// SAP UI5 EventBus instance
const eventBus = EventBus.getInstance();

/**
 * 1. Centralized State (Single Source of Truth)
 * Shared JSONModel for filter state across the application
 */
const filterModel = new JSONModel({
  timeRange: null,
  category: [],
  status: []
});

this.getView().setModel(filterModel, "filters");


/**
 * 2. FilterBar → Update State + Publish Event
 */
onSearch() {
  const filters = this.getFilterValues();

  // Update centralized state
  this.getModel("filters").setData(filters);

  // Notify other components (no payload coupling)
  eventBus.publish("app.filters", "changed", {
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  });
}


/**
 * 3. Dashboard → React to State Change
 */
onFiltersChanged() {

  // Always read from centralized state (source of truth)
  const filters = this.getModel("filters").getData();

  // Update all Integration Cards (widgets)
  this._cards.forEach((card) => {
    card.setParameters(filters);
    card.refresh(); // triggers data re-fetch + re-render
  });
}

Pros:

Single Source of Truth: Centralized filter state

Explicit Notifications: EventBus for change observation

Universal Support: Works with Component and Declarative cards

Debuggable: Inspect model state + trace events

Testable: Mock state and events independently

 

Widget-to-Dashboard Communication

Widgets fire action events on user interaction

{
  "sap.card": {
    "type": "List",
    "content": {
      "item": {
        "title": "{name}",
        "actions": [
          {
            "type": "Custom",
            "parameters": {
              "action": "viewDetails",
              "id": "{id}",
              "name": "{name}"
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

Host (Dashboard) handle it

oCard.attachAction((oEvent) => {
  const action = oEvent.getParameter("action");
  const params = oEvent.getParameter("parameters");

  if (action === "Custom" && params.action === "viewDetails") {
    // handle widget interaction
    console.log("Item clicked:", params);

    eventBus.publish("widget.item", "selected", params);
  }
});

Flow:

  1. User clicks item in widget
  2. Widget fires action event
  3. Dashboard receives event
  4. Dashboard updates filter state
  5. Dashboard publishes filter change
  6. All widgets refresh with new filter

 

Lessons Learned

What Worked Well

Two-repository approach

  • True team independence
  • No merge conflicts

Deploy anytime without coordination

SAPUI5 Integration Cards

  • Standardized pattern
  • Supports Component and Declarative types
  • Built-in features reduce boilerplate

Hybrid communication

  • Centralized state = single source of truth
  • EventBus = loose coupling
  • Works with all card types

Contract-based development

  • Registry acts as interface definition
  • Tags enable dynamic filtering
  • Clear responsibilities

 

Results & Key Takeaways

Achieved:

  • 40+ widgets by 10+ teams
  • Independent deployment cycles
  • Zero downtime deployments

Key Insights:

  • Micro-frontends are organizational—they're about team autonomy and deployment independence, not just technology.
  • Match patterns to constraints—we chose Integration Cards because teams knew UI5, not because it's universally "best."
  • Centralize wisely—filters are centralized (single source of truth), implementation is distributed (team autonomy).
  • Contracts are your API—treat the widget registry with the same rigor as REST APIs.

 

Resources

SAPUI5 Documentation:

  • UI5 Integration Cards
  • EventBus API
  • Component Cards

Micro-frontend Patterns:

  • Micro Frontends by Martin Fowler

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule health checks
entry.content:
As a UX specialist in the SAP S/4HANA Regional Implementation Group, my team and I often get to work with customers keen to leverage the significant capabilities of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule.

With so much interest in Joule and so many use cases available, our aim is to make it easier for you to configure your use cases efficiently and support your business users as they start to use Joule every day.

Good news! In this blog we cover simple checks to ensure that your SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, and Joule architecture is correctly configured and working properly before you onboard your business users.

Performing these checks will allow you to become more confident and leverage the significant benefits of Joule.

NOTE 1: There are 2 different build plans for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. Further details on the foundation and standard plans can be found here: SAP Note: 3704047 - Comparison of Joule functionalities while using SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition using foundation plan or standard plan. 

NOTE 2: The simple tests detailed here are intended to be run AFTER SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule have been fully configured.

NOTE 3: We are planning on releasing 3 , excellent, short videos on Joule support and debugging on https://learning.sap.com/ in the near future. Check back here for details!

 

Architecture

Below, you can see the relationship of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, Joule, SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), SAP Cloud Identity Service, (CIS), SAP Identity Authentication Services (IAS) and SAP Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

Understanding this architecture is critical to testing and troubleshooting your landscape!

NOTE: The above architecture diagram does not include the SAP Web Dispatcher as additional component between the SAP Cloud Connector and SAP S/4HANA. If you use an SAP Web Dispatcher then this needs to be included in any troubleshooting.

Prerequisites

Make sure that you have met all prerequisites before you expose content to your launchpad. It is essential that SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule have been configured correctly as per the following:

  • SAP Help: Joule Onboarding Guide

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition - integration with Joule

  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide

  • SAP Note: 3523238 - Navigational and Transactional capabilities with Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

1.    Introduction

You can test if Joule has been technically correctly configured by prompting Joule to fetch information from the backend system, e.g. ‘Display Sales Order 911’ as in the example below:

Sample Joule capability

Based on the reply from Joule, you can proceed as follows:

- If Joule provides answers with specific sales orders -> the technical configuration is fine

- If Joule does not provide answers, this could have multiple causes:

  • Principal Propagation is not working

  • SAP Identity Provisioning service jobs have not run correctly or have run with errors

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition or content exposure is not correct

  • Timeouts in the backend

  • Role assignment is not correct

  • Other errors

In the following sections we will go through the steps associated with checking the above in more detail.

 

2. Principal Propagation

Principal Propagation is the authentication mechanism that SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, Joule use to authenticate users, and it is essential that it is configured properly. You can check if your Principal Propagation configuration is correct as follows:

  • Test from Joule using a transactional capability as mentioned in the Introduction section of this blog

  • In case you have or can easily add an SAP Business Application Studio instance to the Joule subaccount, test the  "S4HANA_PCE_SSO" destination with Principal Propagation configured using curl command as described in this blog : How to check the connectivity to your backend system in Business Application Studio

  • Or, from a browser, you can simulate a Principal Propagation request also with the “foundation” plan of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (NOTE: This test is more technical!):

           Prerequisite: Check your test user has the necessary role to read Common Data Model (CDM) content (role SAP_FLP_EXP_USER enables viewing of exposed content)

           Refer to: SAP Fiori Launchpad: Manage Launchpad Content Exposure

           The steps for the Principal Propagation simulation test are as follows:

            1. Create a copy of the S4HANA_PCE_SSO destination and call it ‘authtest’

            2. In this new destination ‘authtest’, Add parameter “HTML5.DynamicDestination = true”

            3. An example of the new ‘authtest’ destination is shown below:

Copied destination for Principal Propagation test

                 NOTE: After Principal Propagation has been fully validated, please DELETE the ‘authtest’ destination

            4. The CDM URL is either one of the following, depending on what version of CDM you are using:

                 V1: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities

                 V2: /sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities

                 Version 2 Content Exposure is preferred if this option is available on your system.

                 (NOTE: Make a note of the V1 or V2 URL for use in subsequent steps)

            5. Check the CDM service directly on the SAP S/4HANA system, open the local FLP and test the CDM URL:

Test the CDM URL

 

              6. Open the SAP Build Work Zone instance & copy the entire URL right up to the first ‘/’ and paste in Notepad:

              7. Remove the ‘dt.’ from the URL in the previous step

              8. Add ‘/dynamic_dest/authtest’ to the end of the URL. Note: ‘authtest’ is the copied destination we created in step 1

              9. Construct the URL to test CDM service passing through SAP Build Work Zone and the SAP Cloud Connector:

 

                   Successful Principal Propagation test

 

           10. Successful execution of this URL proves Principal Propagation is working correctly!

Principal Propagation troubleshooting tools are as follows:

       (a) SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition transactions:

                - SMICM (activate HTTP trace level 1)

                - CERTRULE (check rule-based mappings)

                Refer to SAP Note: 2351619 - How to take SMICM trace?

         (b) SAP Cloud Connector logs

Refer to How to troubleshoot Cloud Connector principal propagation over HTTPS

NOTE: We are currently updating our Principal Propagation enablement content, check back here for details!

 

3. SAP Cloud Connector

When you are testing Principal Propagation (in #2), you could consider to temporarily turn on SAP Cloud Connector logs (at a suitably appropriate time of low system usage) which will detect errors such as:

  • Principal Propagation and authentication issues

  • Backend server connectivity issues (e.g., incorrect hostname or protocol, expired certificates)

  • Service issues (e.g., inactive or not found)

In the example below, you can see that authentication has failed due to an expired certificate.

SAP Cloud Connector - expired certificate

 If an error does not show up as expected, you can consider increasing the trace level of the logs as shown below. For SSL issues, a TLS trace has to be active, and for other issues, you need to look at the tunnel trace.

SAP Cloud Connector - trace levels

You should also turn on SAP Cloud Connector Alerting so you can be kept informed about critical system issues (expiring certificates, disk space, CPU load etc…) BEFORE THEY OCCUR.

THIS IS ESPECIALLY IMPORTANT IN YOUR PRODUCTION LANDSCAPE!!!!!

EXAMPLE: By turning on Alerts for expiring certificates, an email alert would be sent 30 days before a certificate expires, so a new certificate can be generated and used, thereby preventing any connectivity issues:

Refer to SAP BTP Connectivity - Alerting

 

4. Check your content exposure

You have already run transaction code /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE to expose your chosen roles to SAP BTP. Refer to:

  • SAP Help: Manage Launchpad Content for Exposure

  • Blog: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide

Now, you can check if your content has been successfully exposed and is ready for consumption by SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

Exposure is available in two versions: the current Version 1 and the new Version 2 as follows:

  • Version 1 is based on legacy target mappings

  • Version 2 provides a seamless end-to-end app model, extending from the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library to business user usage. Version 2 is based on Launchpad App Descriptor Items (LADIs) and is the long-term, future-proof successor to Version 1.

NOTE: Since apps have different IDs in version 2, there is no compatible upgrade path from version 1 to version 2. Implementing integration using version 2 requires initiating a new project.

Depending on whether you are using Version 1 or Version 2, the following URLs can be used to test your content exposure to SAP BTP:

  • Version 1 http://<your-server-name>:<your-port-number>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities

  • Version 2 http://<your-server-name>:<your-port-number>/sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities

Version 1 and 2 have different URLs, 1 is based on classical tiles and target mappings and 2 is based on LADIs.

The URLs must be run in the browser (not via SAP BTP) and expose content in JSON format. Below, you can see that SAP Fiori App F0233A using semantic object ‘OutboundDelivery’ and action ‘displayFactSheet’ has successfully been exposed from a backend SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system and can  be read by SAP BTP.

Test the url directly in a  browser (not via SAP BTP) with the system user configured in the design time destination (JOULE_ADMIN in the configuration guide).

Testing content exposureRefer to:

  • SAP Note: 3501486 - Launchpad Content Exposed with version 2 not updated in SAP Build Workzone, Standard edition

  • SAP Note: 3345119 - Expose Launchpad Content to SAP BTP - Exposure Version 2

  • SAP Help: Manage Launchpad Content for Exposure

 

5. SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Now you can check your Content Channel, and the associated destinations work correctly

  • Check that the Design Time and Runtime destinations (‘jouledt’ and ‘joulert’) work successfully:Destination TestsExample: An incorrect Design Time destination will cause errors importing roles from SAP S/4HANA Cloud  Private Edition. NOTE: The Ping does *not* fully check the entire destination. If it is “red” you know something is wrong, but “green” does not mean that everything is fine

  • You can check if your Content Channel synchronization works successfully (NOTE: the synchronization will not work if  the destination pings in (a) have not been successfully executed). Click on the ‘Synch’ button to consume content exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition:

  • Click on the ‘Report’ link to check that there are no synch errors:

  •  Check the Content Explorer to ensure that content exposed through a channel has been successfully consumed by SAP BTP- From the Site Manager page, select Content Manager and then Content Explorer:

  • Chose the Channel through which your content was consumed by SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition:

  • Here you can see the content consumed through your selected channel. In this example, you can see that 31 roles have been exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and consumed by SAP BTP.   

  • Check roles exposed from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition have been successfully consumed by SAP BTP:

  • Check that your test user has the correctly exposed roles assigned:

        NOTE: If you do not see any roles here, check the following:

  • The test user has the necessary roles in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

  • Roles have been exposed via Content Exposure

  • SAP Identity Provisioning service jobs have run and completed successfully

Refer to:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition - troubleshooting

  • Integration Guide: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, Standard Edition Integration with SAP S/4HANA

 

6. Identity Provisioning service

The navigation service of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition uses the SAP Identity Provisioning service (IPS). IPS provisions identities and their authorizations between source and target systems. To use the navigational capabilities in Joule, you need to configure SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition as the source and SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition as the target system in IPS. Here, you confirm successful provisioning by reviewing logs and status of synchronizing jobs.

- In SAP Cloud Identity Services, view Identity Provisioning -> Provisioning Logs:

Provisioning logs

- Now you can see the Job Execution Logs and check them to ensure there are no errors:

Execution logs

 - In this example, you can see that user BPINST cannot be provisioned correctly by SAP IPS as it has NO email address -> this  user will experience issues with SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule.

Example execution log

7.    Joule Welcome

Once all the above tests have been successfully executed, you can run Joule. If Joule has been configured correctly then there will be a welcome screen with Joule skills showing as in the example below:

Joule debugger console

Click on the debug icon shown above to check there are no errors in the debugger console:

Now you can go ahead and start using Joule and leverage its business value!

 

8. Help! I have tried all the above checks and still have problems

Don’t worry! There are lots of great troubleshooting tools.

a)  Check if the related Fiori apps on the SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad are working without issues:

      App Support: Troubleshoot configuration and authorization errors for apps in the SAP Fiori launchpad.

     Refer to:

  • App Support
  • Setting Up App Support

       App Inspector: Troubleshoot configuration and authorization errors for apps available through the SAP Fiori Launchpad

  Refer to:

  • App Inspector
  • Setting Up App Inspector

b) In Joule, you have the Debug button (click on the ‘Debug’ button show below):

  • Highlights service issues and general errors
  • Active conversation with Joule
  • Response Payload: Shows aggregated JSON response for each Joule interaction
  • Requests Logs: Shows requests and responses grouped by prompt

Joule debug console

 c)  SAP Cloud Connector Logs. These logs can have the following use cases:

  • Principal propagation and authentication troubleshooting
  • Backend server connectivity (e.g., incorrect hostname or protocol, expired certificates)
  • Service issues (e.g., inactive or not found)

 The logs can be accessed in the SAP Cloud Connector as shown:

SAP Cloud Connector logs

Refer to SAP Help: Cloud Connector Trace and Logging

d) SAP Gateway Error Log

  • Transaction /IWFND/ERROR_LOG
  • Shows service errors (e.g., forbidden, not found, inconsistent calls)

      SAP Gateway Tracing Tools

  • Transaction /IWFND/TRACES
  • Troubleshoot service issues, capture header and payload data

      Internet Communication Manager Monitor

  • Transaction SMICM
  • Detailed HTTP calls (by increasing trace level to level 3)
  • Helps to identify authentication and SSL issues

       Refer to SAP Help: Gateway Tracing Tools

Still stuck? Refer to SAP Help: Joule - Monitoring and Troubleshooting which includes details on raising SAP incidents.

Troubleshooting:

  • Blog: Common configuration issues: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • SAP Help: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition, standard edition - Troubleshooting
  • SAP Help: How to troubleshoot Cloud Connector principal propagation over HTTPS
  • SAP Help: Joule Constraints
  • SAP Note: 3649670 - Missing Joule icon in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition Standard Edition
  • SAP Note: 3666014 - Information required for Joule
  • SAP Note: 3673511 - Troubleshooting for Joule page display issues
  • SAP Note: 3679363 - How to recognize a Joule response's

Becoming an SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on the community topic page for SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Other helpful links in the SAP Community:

  • Follow our tag S4HANA RIG - SAP Community for more from the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

entry.title: Running Hugging Face AI Models with SAPUI5 and CAPM
entry.content:
Hi everyone !

After spending several hours researching and experimenting, I finally found a simple way to answer a question that many SAP developers have started asking:

  “Is it possible to run AI models inside SAP applications using SAPUI5 and CAPM?”

The short answer is:

   Yes, absolutely!

In this blog, I will share how we can integrate Hugging Face AI models with SAPUI5 and CAPM, and how we can build a simple AI-powered application that can generate text responses and even images.

First Question: What is Hugging Face?

 If you are new to AI development, you might wonder:

What exactly is Hugging Face?

Think of Hugging Face as GitHub for AI models.

It is a platform where developers and researchers publish and share machine learning models.

Currently, Hugging Face provides access to hundreds of thousands of AI models that can perform tasks such as:

  • Text generation
  • Chatbots
  • Image generation
  • Speech recognition
  • Translation
  • Code generation

The best part is that you can use these models directly through APIs without training your own model.

Another great advantage is that Hugging Face provides GPU infrastructure, which means you can run advanced models without owning expensive hardware.

And yes — if you build your own model, you can also publish it on Hugging Face and make it available globally.

        Fig 1.1 Hugging Face Models sections

 

Now the Interesting Part: Can SAPUI5 Use AI?

When I started this experiment, my first question was:

Can SAPUI5 directly call an AI model?

The answer is yes, especially for text generation models.

To make this work, we only need three things:

-  A Hugging Face account
-  An HF API Token
-  A model endpoint

Once you log in to Hugging Face, you can generate an HF Token from your account settings.

Then you can choose any model depending on your use case. Hugging Face provides model benchmarks and descriptions, so you can easily decide which one is best for:

  • Education
  • Coding
  • Reasoning
  • Chat
  • Content generation

Connecting Hugging Face with SAPUI5

Now let's talk about the implementation.

In the SAPUI5 controller, we can send a request to the Hugging Face API using JavaScript fetch().

The UI simply takes a user prompt, sends it to the AI model, and displays the response.

Example flow:

User enters a prompt:

       "What is Hugging Face and SAPUI5?"

SAPUI5 sends the prompt to the Hugging Face model.

The model generates a response and returns it.

Then SAPUI5 displays the result directly on the UI.

This makes it very easy to build AI chat assistants inside SAP applications.

         Fig 1.2 Output- Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

Sample Code:

      Fig 1.3 sapui5 Controller.js - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

        Fig 1.3 sapui5 view.xml - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

But What About Image Generation?

This is where things become interesting.

When I tried running image generation models directly in SAPUI5, I realized something important.

⚠️SAPUI5 works best with text-based AI APIs.

For image generation models, we need a backend service.

This is where CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model) becomes very useful.

 

 

Using CAPM for Image Generation

To generate images, we can integrate fal.ai, which provides inference APIs for advanced image generation models.

Some examples include:

  • black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev
  • Tongyi-MAI / Z-Image-Turbo

These models can generate high-quality AI images from text prompts.

For example:

"Portrait of a futuristic astronaut with glowing blue eyes"

The system sends this prompt to the AI model, and the model generates an image.

 

How CAPM Handles This

Inside the CAPM Node.js service, we create a custom action such as:

     "generateImage"

This action receives the prompt from SAPUI5.

Then CAPM calls the fal.ai API with that prompt.

The AI model generates the image and returns a URL of the generated image, which we send back to the UI.

This makes CAPM act as a bridge between SAPUI5 and AI models.

 

Why Use fal.ai Instead of Hugging Face Directly?

You might ask:

       If we already use Hugging Face, why do we need fal.ai?

          Good question.

Some models listed on Hugging Face are actually hosted through different inference providers, including fal.ai.

For example:

black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev

This model is often executed through fal.ai inference APIs.

So fal.ai becomes an easy way to run those models with strong GPU performance.

Sample Code:

 

Fig 1.4 Output – CAPM - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.5 service.js - first half- Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.6 service.js - second half - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.7 Fal.ai API key Generation - Hugging face Image generation

 

Final Result

After connecting everything together, we get a simple AI application:

The application can:

- Generate AI text responses using SAPUI5
- Generate AI images using CAPM services
- Use Hugging Face models
- Run without training your own model

All of this can be done using free developer APIs, unless you need large-scale GPU workloads.

 

Conclusion

From this experiment, we can see that SAP applications can easily integrate with modern AI platforms.

The architecture becomes very simple:

                   SAPUI5
                        ⬇
           CAPM Service
                      ⬇
   Hugging Face / fal.ai AI Models

 

Using this approach, developers can build powerful applications such as:

  • AI chat assistants inside SAP
  • AI-powered knowledge systems
  • Image generation tools
  • Smart enterprise automation

And the best part is that you can even deploy your own AI model on Hugging Face and use it inside SAP applications.

 

I hope this small experiment helps other developers who are curious about AI + SAP integration.

Happy coding!

entry.title: Integrating a Custom AI Model into SAP CAP Using AI Launchpad
entry.content:
As we all know AI is spreading across industries like wildfire and a lot of companies want to bring their own custom models into their SAP setups. In this blog I will walk you through a hands-on example of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core. And then making it a callable service in SAP AI Launchpad and connecting it directly to a CAP backend.

Rather than relying on pre-built generative AI services, we demonstrate how to bring your own model, package it in Docker, register it in AI Launchpad, and call it from your backend using a deployment ID. The goal is to cover the whole journey from serving the model to getting real-time results right in your UI.

Requirements

If you are not running everything locally, you'll need admin roles for SAP AI Launchpad and AI Core. For a local setup, there's a build step. For running the trained model in Docker a GPU is recommended but not strictly mandatory. (A CPU will work, just slower) Make sure your machine has enough CPU power, memory and disk space. If you want GPU acceleration, you’ll need a compatible NVIDIA GPU, the right drivers, and CUDA support.

Getting Data Set For Model Training

You can grab image datasets from Roboflow, Kaggle, Google Open Images, or other publicly available sources. But if you plan to use your model for more than just experimenting, you have to check the licenses and usage rights carefully. Or just make your own dataset by collecting and labeling images for your actual use case. That way you’ve got control over both quality and compliance.

Running the Application Locally

The application is first run locally to verify that it works as expected after pushing the model to Docker.

The Dockerfile used for local testing

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the CAP application, the requires section in package.json is updated as follows:

"cds": {
  "sql": {
  },
  "requires": {
    "[production]": {
    },
    "[hybrid]": {
    },
    "barcode-ai": {
      "kind": "rest",
      "credentials": {
        "url": "http://localhost:8080",
        "requestTimeout": 300000
      }
    }
  },
  "auth": "xsuaa",
  "html5-runtime": true,
  "portal": true
},

On the UI side, users will upload barcode images through a FileUploader component.

<u:FileUploader
                    id="imageUploader"
                    name="myFileUpload"
                    fileType="jpg,jpeg,png"
                    placeholder="{i18n>selectBarcodeImg}"
                    change="onImageSelected"
                    width="300px" />

                <Button
                    text="{i18n>barcodeImgTrigger}"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAnalyzeBarcode"
                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop" />

The controller then adds the uploaded image to a FormData object and fires off a POST request.

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Test: Please Select An Image");
        return;
      }

      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const formData = new FormData();
        formData.append("image", this._selectedFile);

        const response = await fetch(
          "/catalog/RoleSearchService/analyze-barcode",
          {
            method: "POST",
            body: formData
          }
        );

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Test: Cant Call Ai Srv");
        }

        const result = await response.json();
        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)
        );
      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Test: Barcode Srv Failed");
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

For local testing the service call in service.js points to your locally running API. With this end-to-end local test you can check if the API can spot barcodes in uploaded images. And if so, pull out their locations, values, types and confidence scores.

The request-response structure of the local test service

 

AI Launchpad Connections

Once you've pushed your project that includes the Dockerfile, serve.py, requirements.txt, and serving_template.yaml to the GitHub, head over to SAP AI Launchpad.

Application settings in AI Launchpad for local testing

Under SAP AI Core Administration, go to Git Repositories and add your GitHub URL. Once the repository is synced, open All Applications and then create a new application. Pick your repository, set the path to (.), and revision to (main).

With the application synced, move to ML Operations and open Scenarios. You will see the serving_template.yaml is picked up automatically as an executable. The template defines the Docker image to run, the container port, resource requests, and the imagePullSecret pointing to our Docker Hub credentials, which are registered separately under Docker Registry Secrets.

 

 

Next, make a Configuration under the scenario, linking the executable to a resource group.

One important detail here is that the serving_template.yaml must define the metadata annotations, labels, and spec fields as pipe-delimited strings rather than nested YAML objects. This is a requirement of the SAP AI Core ServingTemplate schema that differs from standard Kubernetes manifests.

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: barcode-reader-serving
  namespace: <your-namespace>
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: "barcode-scenario"
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0.0"
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/metric: rps
        autoscaling.knative.dev/target: "100"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: "<your-dockerhub-username>/barcode-reader:latest"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
              protocol: TCP
          command: ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "0.5"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"

 

Once your configuration is set trigger a Deployment. Use the infer.m resource plan, as it gives enough compute. After a few minutes the deployment will reach Running status with a unique Deployment ID.

Deployment Details in SAP AI Launchpad

 


Model binding in the updated package.json

Take that Deployment ID and put it into the cds.requires section of package.json under the barcode-ai destination. Now you can pass base64-encoded images in the request and get back barcode predictions from your CAP service layer.

 

 

 

 

 

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Please choose an image first!");
        return;
      }
      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const arrayBuffer = await this._selectedFile.arrayBuffer();
        const base64 = btoa(
          new Uint8Array(arrayBuffer)
            .reduce((data, byte) => data + String.fromCharCode(byte), '')
        );
        const response = await fetch("/catalog/SIAService/analyzeBarcodeImage", {
          method: "POST",
          headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
          body: JSON.stringify({ image: base64 })
        });
        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Error in Ai srv call");
        }
        const raw = await response.json();
        const result = typeof raw.value === 'string' ? JSON.parse(raw.value) : raw.value;

        if (!result.barcodeValue) {
          this.byId("resultText").setText("No barcode detected in the image");
          return;
        }

        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          `Barcode: ${result.barcodeValue} | Type: ${result.confidence || "-"}`
        );

        MessageToast.show("Barcode Scanned Successfully!");

      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Barcode Analysis Failed: " + err.message);
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

 

this.on('analyzeBarcodeImage', async (req) => {
      try {
        const image = req.data.image;
        if (!image) {
          req.error(400, 'Image is required');
          return;
        }

        const barcodeAI = await cds.connect.to('<Your_Destination>');
        const deploymentId = '<deploymentId>';
        const response = await barcodeAI.send({
          method: 'POST',
          path: `/v2/inference/deployments/${deploymentId}/v2/models/barcode-model/infer`,
          headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'
          },
          data: {
            image: image
          }
        });

        // response.predictions → [{data, type, rect}]
        const predictions = response?.predictions || [];

        if (predictions.length === 0) {
          return JSON.stringify({ barcodeValue: null, confidence: null, message: 'Barcode not found' });
        }

        return JSON.stringify({
          barcodeValue: predictions[0].data,
          confidence: predictions[0].type,
          allResults: predictions
        });

      } catch (err) {
        console.error('analyzeBarcodeImage error:', err);
        req.error(500, 'Barcode inference failed');
      }
    });

 

Once we have everything wired up and the CAP app is running, we head back to the frontend and run our test by uploading two images, one with a barcode and one without.

Barcode Detection In Action Test1

Barcode Detection In Action Test2

Future Improvements

The big takeaway integrating custom models with AI Launchpad is straightforward. And once you’ve plugged it in, you can use it across all kinds of projects with remarkable ease. That smooth integration makes a huge difference.

You can level this up in a bunch of ways. For example fine-tune a model like YOLOv8 for tricky conditions like dim lighting or angled shots or add QR code support with minimal changes or handle damaged barcodes by searching for similar visual embeddings in a database.

Summary

This blog walked through the full integration path of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core and consuming it from a CAP backend. Instead of relying on a managed generative AI service, the model was packaged in Docker, registered in SAP AI Launchpad via a serving template, and called from the CAP backend using a deployment ID over the AI Launchpad Destination.

The result is a working barcode detection feature that runs entirely on SAP infrastructure, with the model hosted and scaled by AI Core and the business logic kept cleanly within the CAP service layer. And as we covered, this is just the start. The architecture is flexible. Fine-tune for complex real world scenarios, tack on QR codes, or deal with scenarios like damaged barcodes using similarity search.

Hopefully, this gives consultants a clear roadmap for moving past generic AI tools and plugging their own models into real world solutions. You're not limited to barcodes either. This blueprint works for custom vision models, language models, whatever you need.

If you have any questions or thoughts, feel free to leave a comment below.

Ege Aksöyek

entry.title: How to Create an Object List Page in SAP Fiori Elements – Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Introduction : SAP Fiori Elements provides predefined floorplans that help developers build enterprise applications quickly with minimal coding. One of the most commonly used floorplans is the Object Page. The Object Page is designed to display detailed information about a single business object in a structured and organized layout. It allows users to view, edit, and manage detailed data while navigating through different sections of related information within the same page.

SAP Fiori Elements Object Page :

Object Page =  A detailed screen that shows complete information about a selected business object.

When a user selects one record from a List Report, the system navigates to the Object Page to show the full details.

Flow of  Analytical List Page:

  1. CDS View
  2. Annotation
  3. Odata service
  4. SAP Fiori Elements ( Analytical List Page) Template
  5. SAP Fiori Application UI

Step 1: Create CDS View and Metadata Extension:

First, create the Core Data Services (CDS) view that exposes the required business data.
After creating the CDS view, create a Metadata Extension and add the necessary UI annotations to define how the data should appear on the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page.

The metadata extension is used to separate the UI annotations from the main CDS view, which helps maintain a cleaner and more structured development approach.

Note: It is assumed that you already know how to create a CDS View and a Metadata Extension in the ABAP environment. Therefore, this article does not cover the step-by-step creation process. Instead, the examples below directly demonstrate the CDS view and Metadata Extension with the required annotations used for the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page.

1. CDS View:

Code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZOLR'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Object List Report'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view ZCDS_ObjectList_Report
  as select from ztest_sales_tab
{
  key vbeln as SalesDocument,

      vkorg as SalesOrg,
      werks as Plant,
      vtweg as DistrChannel,
      @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
      netwr as Amount,
      waerk as Currency,
      audat as CreationDate,
      case substring( audat, 5, 2 )
      when '01' then concat( 'JAN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '02' then concat( 'FEB-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '03' then concat( 'MAR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '04' then concat( 'APR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '05' then concat( 'MAY-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '06' then concat( 'JUN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '07' then concat( 'JUL-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '08' then concat( 'AUG-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '09' then concat( 'SEP-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '10' then concat( 'OCT-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '11' then concat( 'NOV-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '12' then concat( 'DEC-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )

      end   as Month_Year,
       'Purchase Order Title '              as sampleTitle,
      'My Purchase Order for Goods Services' as sampleDes
    
}

2. Meta data Extension:

Code:

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
@Search.searchable: true


@UI.headerInfo: {
 typeName: 'Purchase Order',
 typeNamePlural: 'Purchase OrderS',
 title: {  type: #STANDARD, value: 'sampleTitle' },
 description:{ type: #STANDARD, value: 'sampleDes' }
 //imageUrl: 'image1'

}


annotate view ZCDS_ObjectList_Report with



{


   .facet: [{
          id: 'General',
          purpose: #STANDARD,
          label: 'Purchase Order',
          type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE
      }]
      
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  .lineItem: [{position: 10}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  .identification: [{position: 10  }]
  SalesDocument;
  .lineItem: [{position: 20}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  .identification: [{position: 20  }]
  SalesOrg;
  .lineItem: [{position: 30}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 30 }]
  .identification: [{position: 30  }]
  Plant;
  .lineItem: [{position: 40}]
  .identification: [{position: 40  }]
  DistrChannel;
  .lineItem: [{position: 50}]
  .identification: [{position: 50  }]
  .dataPoint: { title: 'Amount' , criticality: 'CAmount'}
  Amount;
  .lineItem: [{position: 60}]
  .identification: [{position: 60  }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{position: 70}]
  .identification: [{position: 70  }]
  CreationDate;

  .lineItem: [{position: 80,label: 'Month Year'}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 40  }]
  .identification: [{position: 80  }]
  .dataPoint: { title: 'Month & Year' }
  Month_Year;

}

Step 2: Consume the CDS View in an OData Service:

Note: It is assumed that you already know how to consume a CDS View in an OData service. Therefore, this article does not cover the step-by-step process. Instead, the explanation below directly continues with the required configuration and examples.

Go to SEGW  Tcode and open your  Project and  to add your CDS View .

After adding the CDS entity, the Entity Type, Entity Set, and related properties will be generated automatically in the project.

Step 3:  Create SAP Fiori Application using Object Page Template

Follow the steps  :

Open  BAS Tool and  Click on File and Select as New Project from Template  and   chose  List Report Page template .Please provide the necessary details and Finish It.

After completing these steps, the system automatically generates the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page application.

Once all the above steps are completed and the configuration is done, preview the application in SAP Business Application Studio. The application will load, and you will be able to see the Object List Page with the data displayed.

After completing all the configuration steps, preview the application in SAP Business Application Studio. The application opens with the Object List Page, which displays the list of records in a tabular format.

The Object List Page provides the following features:

  • Filter Bar to search data using fields such as:

    • Sales Document

    • Sales Organization

    • Plant

    • Month_Year

  • Table View displaying all the records retrieved from the OData service.

  • Navigation Arrow on each row for accessing detailed information.

Navigation to Object Page :

Each row in the table contains a navigation arrow ( > ) on the right side.

When you click the arrow icon, the application navigates to the Object Page, where detailed information about the selected record is displayed

Example:

List Report → Object Page

5000000001 > opens detailed view

The Object Page shows the complete information of the selected Sales Document.

 

Conclusion: In conclusion, the Object Page in SAP Fiori Elements provides a structured way to display and manage detailed information about a specific business object. By organizing data into sections and enabling actions such as viewing and editing, it improves usability and helps users efficiently work with business data within a single interface.

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues
entry.content:
Are you interested in activating Joule and integrating it seamlessly into your system—but running into errors along the way? In my role as solution architect for our SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group I’ve worked with some of our first Joule customers and would like to share how you can avoid and resolve errors during the activation.

This blog highlights the most common SAP Joule issues encountered during provisioning, booster execution, and system integration.

You’ll also find clear, step-by-step guidance to help you quickly troubleshoot and succeed with your activation.
Please also refer to our SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule Health Check blog to test your activation and configuration is working correctly before using Joule https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-and-joule-health-checks/ba-p/14348321

We will look at the following topics:

  1. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) subaccount / provisioning issues
  2. Joule booster problems
  3. User Experience (UX) / icon appearance issues
  4. SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning service (IPS) and Identity Authentication service (IAS) issues
  5. Restrictions/ constraints
  6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
  7. References
  8. How to contact SAP Support

1. SAP BTP subaccount / provisioning issues

Issue

Solution/Available fix

You have a license for an SAP product that supports Joule integration (such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, etc.) and after creating a subaccount the Joule service is not available in the subaccount entitlement.

Check SAP Help:Data Centers Supported by Joule

The data centre is supported but the entitlements are not available in your global account.

Contact your SAP Account Manager/Executive. 

Refer to SAP Note 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Help:Joule prerequisites

The menu entry "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of your SAP BTP Cockpit.

You are not an administrator of the Global account.

SAP Note 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of SAP BTP Cockpit

SAP Note 2669325 - How to add a user as a SAP Business Technology Platform Global Account Administrator

SAP systems are not visible in SAP BTP under the system landscape.

 

 

Before adding your system, make sure it's not already auto-discovered and listed automatically on the System Landscape ---> Systems page.

Refer to SAP Help: BTP Registering SAP System
SAP Note 3661817 - SAP systems are missing in System Landscape of the BTP Cockpit

 2. Joule Activation/Issues during the execution of the Joule booster

While running the booster configuration for the Joule Service, the prerequisites check failed for the Identity Authentication Tenant.

 An error message is displayed: "Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant."

 

Ensure that the IAS tenant is visible under the global account and configure trust between the Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and the Joule subaccount. 

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

 

 

 

The Joule booster is being run, and you encounter the following error:

*********************************
Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host <IAS tenant>. 
Report an incident in the BC-CP-MP component 

The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant isn't connected to the correct SAP BTP Global Account, or it's linked to the wrong customer ID.
How to Fix it:
a. Make sure your IAS tenant is linked to the correct Global Account.
Check the SAP Help:View Assigned Tenants and Admins.

b. If you have multiple IAS tenants , use the IAS tenant that matches the Global Account's customer ID.
Important:Data syncing between systems usually takes up to one week.Wait for this to complete before running the booster again so that the SAP IAS system appears in all the accounts.

You can manually trigger the sync via the “Refresh” button.
SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

Enabling Joule leads to the following error: 
"Prerequisites for the formation type "Integration with Joule" are not fulfilled.
No SAP Cloud identity service system is showing up under“System landscape” --->System

 

A new SAP BTP Global Account will take some time to synchronize all existing customer systems, but you can manually trigger the sync via the "Refresh" button.
This should ensure that your SAP Cloud Identity Services system shows up. Afterwards you can proceed with the Joule configuration.

 

The SAP Build Work Zone system does not appear in the dropdown list during the setup process (Joule Booster - Step 5 Set Up Integrations).

 

 

The SAP Build Work Zone service was not subscribed to under the relevant subaccount.
An instance of this service is not enough for the integration; therefore, the system will not appear under the options.

Add the SAP Build Work Zone subscription in the Joule subaccount. Refer to
SAP Note 3710864 - SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

Unable to find the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition option in Step 3 - "Select Integration" stage dropdown during activation by means of the Joule Booster.

In global account Systems, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system is already auto-discovered.

Ensure that it is visible in Joule Booster Step 04  "Select Capabilities", select "S/4HANA Private Cloud" and proceed to complete the Joule booster.

Refer to SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster for detailed information.

Error message: "The subscription of subaccount <xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxx> to application das-application and plan foundation, failed with state: SUBSCRIBE_FAILED."

Observe the error messages indicating SaaS Subscription failed and instructions to open a support case or unsubscribe and retry.

 

Refer to SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster for detailed instructions.

If the issue persists, raise a support incident and choose component CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB for further investigation.

 

 

 3. UX/ Icon appearance issue

Joule refused to frame 'https://xxx.xxx.cloud.sap/' because an ancestor violates the following Content Security Policy directive: "frame-ancestors”.                             

 

 

 

 

Enable 3rd party cookies of your browser.Add the trusted domain in SAP IAS and SAP BTP
a. Add trusted domain in SAP IAS in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
Go to Applications & Resources -->Tenant Settings --> Trusted Domains--> Click the + (Add) button
Type the URL of the trusted host into the field.

b. Add the trusted domain in SAP BTP.
In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your specific subaccount ---> Security ---> Settings.
Click the + (Add) button.
Enter the required hostname, for example: https://<your-s4-or-sfsf-url>.com

The Joule icon at the top app bar in the Fiori Launchpad is not properly displayed.

The issue is caused using an unsupported SAPUI5 version in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. 

 

Upgrade the SAPUI5 Component version of SAP S/4HANA to be equal to or higher than the minimum supported version.

SAP Notes:
2346367 - How to upgrade the SAPUI5 library in ABAP - SAP for Me
3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
 

After integrating Joule with SAP Solutions, the user is unable to launch Joule by clicking the diamond icon,  it fails to load or respond.

 

 

Allow third-party cookies.

SAP Note 3643889 - User unable to launch the Joule Icon after integrating with SAP Solutions

The Joule icon is not appearing for a specific user despite that the correct role-based permissions have been assigned in SAP SuccessFactors.

The issue is most likely caused by security policies of the network provider, which may be blocking certain URLs that are required for the icon to appear.
The affected user does not have the General User Permission --->Access to Joule

Refer to SAP Note 3685621 - Joule button not appearing on SuccessFactors

After clicking on the Joule icon in SAP SuccessFactors, it tries to load for a few minutes and subsequently the user gets a blank or greyed-out screen.

Refer to SAP Note 3544482 - Getting Error / White /Blank / IAS login Screen When Trying to Launch Joule in SuccessFactors

4. SAP IPS/IAS issues

The SAP IAS source system read job is failing.

 

Keep the source system name the same as in the content channel that had been created in the standard SAP Build Work Zone.

Joule was successfully integrated with the SAP product. However, clicking the Joule icon results in a blank white or SAP IAS login screen. Single sign-on is not working.

 

Check the SAP IAS domain. In the SAP BTP Joule subaccount, go to Trust Configuration and select the Custom Identity Provider for Applications.

Change the field "Domain" to the one used in the SAP product.

Refer to SAP Note 3635949 - Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

5. Restrictions /Constraints
SAP Joule operates within specific constraints.
SAP notes:
3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
3711505 - Restrictions: Multiple different Work Zone editions integration with Joule
Before starting the Joule project, please refer to the document below. All constraints applicable to Joule are listed here.
SAP Help:Joule constraints

6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
You may experience issues that would have to be reported in SAP incidents. In such cases, please collect information, which will help the support team to investigate and resolve the problem quickly.
SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Jouletroubleshooting
SAP Help:Monitoring and Troubleshooting

7. How to report issues
You can report an incident or error through SAP for Me. Ensure that you provide a detailed description of the issue including the steps to reproduce it. Attach any relevant screenshots, logs, or error messages to help with the investigation. Please use the appropriate components listed below when creating your incident.

General support component for core Joule services issue and functionality

CA_JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

SaaS Subscription error after running Joule Booster

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

8. Frequently asked questions

  1. Are there options available to customize the theme of Joule so it aligns with our branding guidelines and specific colour palette?

The theme tab is only available when Joule is used in a standalone (single page) mode. For now, Joule automatically adapts to the theme of the embedding application.  For example, if the user will changes the theme in the SAP Work Zone settings, Joule will automatically adopt it. The user cannot choose a different theme for the Joule client.
SAP Note 3646989 - Is it possible to adjust theming for Joule?

  1. Is it possible to remove the Joule icon for selected users?

When Joule is enabled through the Joule booster, it is activated for all users in both SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems. At present, there is no supported option to restrict or disable Joule for specific users or user groups.
SAP Note 3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Summary
In this blog, we reviewed common configuration and activation issues related to Joule including challenges with SAP Business Technology Platform provisioning, Joule booster activation, UX visibility, and integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services (IPS/IAS), along with key restrictions, monitoring tips, and reference resources.

In addition, we discussed current system restrictions and limitations, monitoring options, log analysis techniques, and practical troubleshooting steps to help identify and resolve issues efficiently.

By following the guidance and best practices outlined above, administrators can streamline Joule activation, minimize configuration errors, and ensure a smoother user experience. If problems persist, contact SAP Support with detailed information such as error messages, screenshots, tenant details, and relevant logs to accelerate root cause analysis and resolution.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: AI in the Toolbox: Exploring VS Code for Fiori Prompt Generation
entry.content:
Microsoft has rolled out some impressive new coding‑assistance capabilities across its AI offerings. Since VS Code and GitHub are already common tools in Fiori development, it’s great to see them gaining even more intelligence baked in.

In my day‑to‑day work, I still jump between BAS, the old WebIDE (yes—some landscapes still use it!), and VS Code, depending on the client setup and what the project calls for. So it made sense to explore how these new AI features fit into that mix.

This is largely an exploratory exercise—not an attempt to replace Joule. Joule remains SAP’s primary AI platform and will continue to be the preferred option where available. But there are environments where Joule isn’t accessible, and in those cases it’s worth understanding what Microsoft’s tooling can offer.

The following is one use-case for prompt generation that I've been playing with.

Store prompts in .github/prompts/ in your repository. You can type /fiori-smartapp in VSCode so Copilot generates your scaffold.

 

# Name: fiori-smartapp
# Description: Generate a UI5 1.76 CRUD page with SmartFilterBar+SmartTable and controller.

**Task**
Create:
1) `webapp/view/Orders.view.xml` – SmartFilterBar bound to `OrdersSet`, SmartTable (growing=false), toolbar with Refresh, Export.
2) `webapp/controller/Orders.controller.js` – load model, table binding, `onRefresh`, `onExport`, MessageManager error handling, `callFunction` wrapper that correlates batch errors to line items.
3) Unit test skeleton in `webapp/test/unit/Orders.controller.js`.

**Constraints**
- Framework: SAPUI5 1.76 (compatibility mode), OData V2.
- Use stable IDs; add i18n keys for all labels.
- Follow MVC; no busy‑waiting; use Promises.

**Output format**
Provide the 3 files with full code blocks.

The capability stacks well with other organization‑wide tools and use cases, making it extremely useful. It can remove a significant amount of grunt work from the development loop—though it’s clear that humans must remain firmly in the process. I’m genuinely amazed that with only modest investment in strategy and implementation, AI tools can deliver a generational leap in code generation, architecture, QA, refactoring, and documentation.

Happy coding!

entry.title: SAP User Experience Q1/2026 Update – Part 9: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile
entry.content:
This ninth and last post in my series gives an overview innovations in our UI tools and technologies, making it ever easier to design, build and integrate UIs, for both web and mobile applications. For designing apps, web and mobile, we have added AI content and more to the SAP Design System portal and UI Kits in Figma. For web developers, we have introduced the SAP Fiori development portal, as well as more AI support via MCP servers for SAPUI5 and for SAP Fiori elements, and Joule integration. Beyond that: have a look at how you can use AI to migrate to TypeScript, learn about the new Geomap control, and benefit from the new SAPUI5 runtime repository. Finally, discover the latest updates for mobile design and development with our SDKs and the mobile development kit.

Joule can also be seen as a technology for delivering a great user experience. To find out about recent innovations there, as well as for central entry points and services, have a look at this post, which also contains links to all the other parts of the series:

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

In case you missed them, do have a look at my previous posts covering design and technology news, since what I mention here builds partly on what came before:

  • SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile. This post has a lot of information about extensibility, especially for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Do also have a look at the new SAP Extensibility Explorer.
  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile.

First, we’ll have a look at news for creating web UIs, before looking at what’s new for mobile design and development.

 

News for Creating Web UIs

I’ve compiled the news into these sections:

  • SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements.
  • SAP Fiori Development Portal.
  • Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule.
  • SAP Fiori Tools News.

SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements

We recently introduced the new SAP Design System portal that brings together the product design guidelines SAP Fiori for web, SAP Fiori for iOS, and SAP Fiori for Android, as well as to the new digital design system guidelines. With its enhanced usability, simplified versioning and improved performance, designers (and developers!) get a better user experience compared with the previous SAP Fiori design guidelines.

End of last year we introduced these AI UI elements for web UIs, as you can see in Figure 1 below:

  • AI Acknowledgement.
  • AI Button.
  • AI Writing Assistant.

Figure 1: AI UI elements available in SAP Design System portal. Alt Text: Screenshot of the portal UI Elements page with a filter at the top containing “AI”, below that “3 results” showing three cards in a horizontal row for “AI Acknowledgement”, “AI Button” and “AI Writing Assistant”.

Get a complete overview of what’s new in the design system portal here

  • What’s New in the SAP Design System Portal?

UI Kits v8.0 and v9.0 – now with AI components

The new UI Kits available for designers in Figma make it easy for designers to design accessible UIs at enterprise scale, for web applications, mobile iOS and Android applications as well as wearables. The UI Kits provide reusable components for web and mobile, along with web floorplans, which give you what you need out-of-the-box.

In the meantime, we have released SAP Web UI Kit v8.0 which includes a full set of AI components: AI Button, AI Menu Button, AI Split Menu Button, AI Input, and AI Text Area.

The latest release, SAP Web UI Kit v9.0, continues to evolve the system with focused improvements that enhance visual quality, usability, and overall consistency across the kit. It brings:

  • Updated Icons:
    All icons have been refreshed with a 1.5px stroke (down from 1.6px). This improves anti-aliasing and results in cleaner, sharper visuals across interfaces.
  • Busy Indicator Enhancements:
    You now have two Busy Indicator options, static and animated, available on the same page. The new animated version is ready to use out of the box, saving time and implementation effort.
  • Redesigned Color Palette. Note that the previous color palette component has been removed in order to avoid confusion, and keep the kit cleaner and easier to navigate.

Find out more here:

  • New SAP Design System Portal for SAP Fiori Design Guidelines, and New UI Kits for Designers.

SAP Fiori Development Portal

I’m also happy to inform you about the new SAP Fiori development portal. It is designed to help developers maximize the benefits of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4, which is more than just a UI framework: it’s a metadata-driven approach that leverages the SAP Design System as its guiding principle, incorporating the most relevant design system options for typical business tasks.

Figure 2: The SAP Fiori development portal. Alt text: A screenshot showing the Introduction page. On the left a menu has entries Using the Portal, Building Blocks, Global Patterns, Floorplans, SAP Fiori Tools. The Introduction page has a full width card “Building Blocks” at the top, with a description and tiles representing various building blocks. Below that “Choose Your Option” heading above two side-by-side cards for “Floorplans” and “Customer Layout”, each with an image and description, and a “Learn More” button.

The SAP Fiori development portal puts building blocks front and center. These building blocks - such as tables, forms, filter bars, and charts - are orchestrated by SAP Fiori elements and form the basis of every OData V4 application. They provide standardized layouts and interaction patterns, ensuring UI consistency and boosting developer productivity. The great advantage of using building blocks is that you can use them to create a customer layout and yet still benefit from the advantages of the metadata driven approach: low code, built in enterprise-grade capabilities such as multi-language support, accessibility, security and performance, and design system consistency.

Find out more in this six-part series of blog posts, here is the first one, which contains links to the others:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6).

We have also restructured our documentation for SAP Fiori elements, with separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, and better supporting the building block approach. Read more here:

  • SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure.

Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule

The above posts from Q1/2025 and Q3/2025 introduced a number of powerful ways to accelerate UI development by using AI, such as:

  • Creating SAP Fiori elements apps from a sketch (in the Q1 post), now integrated in SAP Build Code and Joule (Q3 post)
  • Joule for SAPUI5 freestyle development (Q1 post)
  • AI-assisted auto-fix of coding errors with UI5 linter (Q3 post)

Recently, we have gone even further and introduced MCP servers for freestyle SAPUI5 development as well as for developing with SAP Fiori elements and the associated building blocks. These open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide AI agents with comprehensive UI5 and SAP Fiori elements knowledge, including best practices and project-aware context information, and thus provide developers with valuable support. Figure 3 shows examples.

Figure 3: Examples of the UI5 MCP Server (on the left) and the MCP Server for SAP Fiori (on the right) in action. Alt Text: both images show screenshots of a CLINE console view with a task entered as text at the top, followed by a list of steps which the system proposes to do to solve the task.

Read about these new MCP servers in these blog posts:

  • UI5 MCP Server.
  • MCP Server for SAP Fiori.
  • Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits

Alternatively, you can listen to these podcasts involving Tim Back and Florian Vogt, talking about the intricacies of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) within the SAP ecosystem:

  • Spotify: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.
  • Apple Podcast: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.

Latest UI5 MCP Server Enhancements

The UI5 MCP server got extended to help migrate existing JavaScript code to TypeScript, as well as for generating UI Integration Cards.

New TypeScript Migration Feature

With the newest feature of the UI5 MCP server, UI5-specific TypeScript conversion assistance is now available to AI coding agents, making the task much easier. Find out more in this blog post:

  • UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices

Generating UI Integration Cards

The UI5 MCP Server now supports the development of UI Integration Cards. In its latest version – v0.2.1 – it includes three new tools dedicated to UI Integration Cards:

  1. Best practice guidance for your agent.
  2. Scaffolding a new Integration Card.
  3. Validating the Card’s manifest.

These tools assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Find out more here:

  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation.

AI News for SAP Fiori Tools and MCP Server for SAP Fiori

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Joule Enhancement: Create Freestyle SAPUI5 Apps in SAP Build Code

You can now use Joule in SAP Build Code to create new freestyle SAPUI5 apps according to your requirements. The feature is not absolutely groundbreaking, as there are other tools doing a similar job like the Easy-UI5 templates and the recently published UI5 MCP Server. But:

  1. It brings the functionality right to the fingertips of developers using SAP Build Code – no other tools needed.
  2. It extends the template capabilities beyond what the Easy-UI5 templates provide.
  3. Implementing it within the Build Code Joule environment yielded interesting insights and further improvements.

Find out more about the feature, and in the second “making of” post, read about how Andreas Kunz went about building it, and what he learnt from doing it:

  • Joule in SAP Build Code can now create freestyle UI5 apps.
  • Inside Joule: Lessons from building an AI coding assistant for SAP Build.

SAP Fiori Tools News

In addition to the AI-enhancements mentioned above, we now provide expanded support for SAP Fiori elements building blocks, a new connection manager for SAP Systems in VS Code, and a feature to preview SAP Fiori web apps on mobile devices.

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

The following enhancements make it easier to adapt applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including

  • rich text editors within custom sections,
  • custom columns for table building blocks, and
  • the page building block itself.

Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor.

To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

We have introduced the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension for managing connections to remote SAP systems. The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools.

Preview SAP Fiori Web Apps on Mobile

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices. You do this by using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher and creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access. When you start the run configuration, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

Find out more here:

  • SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity.
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38).
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #39 (March 2026).

 

News for Mobile Designers and Developers

Let’s first look at updates in the design guidelines and the SDKs, and then look at news for the mobile development kit (MDK).

Mobile Design Guideline and SDK Updates

We have introduced numerous new and enhanced patterns and components this last half-year. I have selected a small number to highlight here, further below you’ll find links to get the full overview.

New for AI

The following two new patterns have been introduced for both the SAP Fiori for Android and the SAP Fiori for iOS design guidelines.

AI Insights Pattern: We have introduced a new AI Insights Pattern, which describes how to present AI insights optimally on mobile devices, as shown in Figure 4. It provides a standardized way to display a summary, analysis, or suggestion generated with AI. This pattern can be applied to a full-screen or a card depending on the scope of content generated by AI.

AI Handoff Pattern: We have created a new guideline describing the AI Handoff Pattern, which defines how a user should transition between contextual embedded AI features and the Joule conversational panel.

Figure 4: Two new AI patterns: AI Insights Pattern and AI Handoff Pattern. Alt Text: On the left two image for AI Insights Pattern, first a full mobile phone screen with title and subtitle at the top, followed by two insights sections, each with two slots for content, at the bottom a user feedback row with thumbs up and thumbs down icons, above a an AI generate button. On the right a diagram for the AI Handoff Pattern showing a schematic mobile module with arrow to and from a schematic Joule UI.

Further news

I have picked two stand-out updates, also made available for Android and iOS.

New Illustrations: We have introduced the new illustrations, that were also introduced in the web, to the mobile design system and the mobile SDKs. You can see a few examples on the left in Figure 5 below.

More flexible card layouts: We offer far more flexibility now in how you use cards to summarize information. For example, you can create wider cards that have the image on the left or the right, and the content block next to it. With the new flex item component, the card’s main header can now be customized to hold more content by adding tags and status info labels outside, on top, between, or below the title and subtitle. Two examples are shown in Figure 5 on the right.

Figure 5: New illustrations and more flexible card layout and content options. Alt Text: On the left three different-size “My Daily Tasks” cards, with images for “You’r all caught up”, “Oops” and “Congratulations”. On the right two card examples: at the top a slim wide card with title, subtitle and one line text on the left, image on the right; below that a larger card with image on the left, title and details with colored indicators and buttons on the right.

Find out more here:
